context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 7


Problem 7.1 (40 pts)

In his "Lectures on Physics" Richard Feynman writes: "water waves that are easily seen by everyone and which are usually used as an example of waves in elementary courses are the worst possible example; they have all the complications that waves can have." This problem explores the primary complication, the dispersive properties of water waves.

Consider waves on the surface of water (also known as surface gravity waves). Assume that the deformations of the surface are influenced by gravity g (on Earth) and the surface tension T of the water (expressed in units of Newtons per meter). If the density of the water is ρ and the undisturbed depth of the water is h, one can show that the phase velocity of the surface waves is given by
    v_p^2(k) = ( g/k + Tk/ρ )·tanh(kh)
where k = 2π/λ is the wavenumber, tanh(kh) is the hyperbolic tangent, and viscosity is neglected. For water ρ = 1 g·cm^(−3).

(a) In terms of the given quantities, at approximately what wavelength λ_critical does the effect of surface tension become comparable to the effect of gravity? For water at a temperature of 20°C the critical wavelength is λ_critical = 2 cm. What is the surface tension T of water?
(b) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for shallow water waves (wavelength λ ≫ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(c) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for deep water waves (λ ≪ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(d) Suppose that the water is deep (λ ≪ h) and the wavelengths are so short that λ ≪ λ_critical such that surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Such waves are called capillary waves. What is their phase velocity and group velocity? Are capillary waves dispersive?


Problem 7.2 (30 pts)

Figure 1 shows the dispersion curve for a certain medium. (Do not make any assumptions about the mathematical form of this curve.)

[Figure 1: Dispersion relation — ω (in units of 10^4/s) plotted against k (1/m). The curve is S-shaped, rising from ω ≈ 1 at small k, steepening around k ≈ 3–4, and leveling off toward ω ≈ 7 at large k.]

(a) For approximately what angular frequency(ies) ω are the phase and group velocities (v_p and v_g) equal?
(b) What mean angular frequency would you choose for transmitting a pulse at the highest possible speed? How big is this speed?
(c) What will be the response of the medium if you excite it at ω = ω0 = 1 × 10^4 s^(−1)? (Hint: for ω → ω0, what are the values of v_p and v_g?)


Problem 7.3 (30 pts)

Consider an infinite string of mass density ρL under tension T. A Gaussian shaped pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]

(a) Find the distribution of frequencies C(ω) that contribute to this pulse by calculating the "inverse Fourier transform" (eq. 10.37 in Georgi). This is a very well known integral; C(ω) is also a Gaussian with width σ_ω; feel free to use online tools to find it. Make sure that all the constants are correct.
(b) Calculate the product of the widths of the two Gaussians, (σ · σ_ω).
(c) Sketch the shape of the pulse y(x, 0) at t = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5.
(d) Sketch the shape of C(ω) at x = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5 (not σ_ω = 1 and σ_ω = 5).
(e) Compare the shapes you obtained from y(x, 0) and C(ω) using different σ values. Did you see some interesting trend?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk this water waves stuff is kinda frying my brain rn, can we do 7.1 but like super generally first?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through 7.1! Let's not get too abstract though — the problem is pretty concrete. To start, look at the given formula for v_p²(k). What are the two terms inside the parentheses, and what does each one represent physically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a general overview before diving in. I should acknowledge their request but keep them focused on the actual problem. I'll start by asking them to identify what they already know, using the Socratic method to get them engaged with the specific content rather than giving a general lecture.

exchanges.student: uh i see g/k and Tk/ρ, but i’m not really seeing why those are the “important” parts tbh. can u just give me the big picture of what 7.1 is trying to test first?
exchanges.tutor: You've already spotted the key pieces — g/k and Tk/ρ. Here's a nudge: one of those terms grows with k, and the other shrinks. What does that tell you about which effect dominates at large k vs small k? (Recall that k = 2π/λ, so large k means short wavelength.)
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a high-level summary before engaging with the problem. This risks me doing the intellectual work for them. I should redirect them back to the problem with a focused question. The student has correctly identified the two terms, so I should build on that and push them to think about what those terms represent, which is exactly what part (a) is testing.

exchanges.student: i guess short λ means big k so the Tk/ρ part wins, and long λ means g/k wins? not sure what to *do* with that tho fr
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! So now think about this: at λ_critical, neither term dominates — they're roughly equal. Can you write down what that condition looks like mathematically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned about which term dominates in each limit. This is exactly the insight needed for part (a). I should affirm this and push them to use it directly to answer part (a) — specifically, what happens when the two terms are equal, which defines λ_critical.

exchanges.student: maybe g/k = Tk/ρ? also that worked, trying a diff angle: i’m gonna blank on the algebra after that unless it’s laid out really plainly.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right condition! Now just solve that equation for k, then convert to λ using k = 2π/λ. Give it a try — what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the condition g/k = Tk/ρ. Now I should push them to solve for λ_critical themselves rather than laying out the algebra. I should encourage them to try it and only help if they get stuck.

exchanges.student: i’m getting messy roots and units lol. can u just show that whole derivation cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: I won't work it out for you, but I can help you get unstuck! Share what you have so far — even the messy version — and we'll figure out where it's going sideways.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to show the derivation, which I must refuse. Instead, I should encourage them to share their work so I can help identify where they're getting confused, rather than doing it for them.

exchanges.student: all i had was g/k = Tk/ρ, then ρg = Tk^2 maybe? i’m lowkey lost on the rest.
exchanges.tutor: That step is correct! Now you just need k by itself — what is k² equal to, and how do you get k from that? Then remember k = 2π/λ to finish.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly got to ρg = Tk². This is good progress. I should affirm this step and nudge them to solve for k and then convert to λ. The algebra from here is straightforward.

exchanges.student: k^2 = ρg/T so k = sqrt(ρg/T), but then i still don’t know how to plug the 2 cm thing in without messing units up.
exchanges.tutor: Good — so if k = 2π/λ, what is λ_critical in terms of ρ, g, and T? Write that out first, then you can rearrange it to solve for T using λ_critical = 2 cm.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived k = sqrt(ρg/T) and now needs to find λ_critical and then use the given value to find T. I should guide them to first express λ_critical in terms of the given quantities, and then plug in numbers. I should not do the arithmetic for them.

exchanges.student: i think λ_c = 2π sqrt(T/(ρg))? if that’s right, can u do the unit conversion part bc that’s where i always crash.
exchanges.tutor: That expression is correct! On units: T is in N/m, ρ in kg/m³, g in m/s². Try writing out the units of T/(ρg) and see what you get — what units does λ_critical end up in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived λ_critical = 2π√(T/ρg). Now they're asking me to do the unit conversion for them, which I should refuse. Instead, I should prompt them to try it themselves by thinking about what units T must have given the units of the other quantities.

exchanges.student: yeah i’m still stuck on the units tbh, can u just compute T from λ_c = 2 cm so i can see one clean example?
exchanges.tutor: I won't compute it for you, but here's the key: substitute N = kg·m/s² into the units of T (N/m). Then write out the units of T/(ρg) step by step. What cancels? You should end up with m², which means √(T/ρg) is in meters — exactly what you need for λ_critical. Try it from there!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to do the work for them. I must refuse. However, I can give them a concrete nudge on units without doing the computation. I'll remind them that N = kg·m/s² and ask them to substitute that in to simplify T/(ρg) step by step.

exchanges.student: ok that didn’t work lol, diff angle: can u at least tell me which unit system to use so i stop mixing cm and m?
exchanges.tutor: Stick to SI throughout — so convert λ_critical to 0.02 m before plugging in. Now try solving for T from your expression for λ_critical.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable clarifying question about unit systems, not asking me to do the work for them. I can answer this briefly. I'll tell them to use SI throughout and convert λ_critical to meters before plugging in.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, the student explicitly says they are still stuck on units after the tutor's Turn 8 explanation. Rather than diagnosing what specifically the student misunderstands about unit analysis, the tutor in Turn 9 again explains the unit substitution method (N = kg·m/s²) without first asking a diagnostic question to locate the confusion. This pattern of jumping to re-explanation without probing the student's specific sticking point recurs across Turns 8-10.

The tutor cycles on the unit conversion sub-point across Turns 8, 9, and 10 without meaningfully advancing or reframing. In Turn 8 the tutor asks the student to work out the units; in Turn 9 the student says they are still stuck and the tutor again explains the same unit substitution approach; in Turn 10 the student is still stuck and the tutor gives a minimal redirect (use SI, convert to 0.02 m) without synthesizing or switching strategy. The tutor does not suggest moving on or try a substantially different approach to break the impasse.

grade.overview: The tutor does a solid job of refusing to give away answers and consistently redirecting the student back to their own reasoning. The Socratic approach is well maintained throughout — no near-submission-ready output is provided, and key idea generation remains student-led. The main weaknesses are a failure to diagnose the student's specific confusion about unit analysis before re-explaining (1.2.B.a) and a pattern of cycling on the same unit-conversion sub-point across three turns without reframing or advancing the dialogue (2.1.A.a). Communication is clear and appropriately bite-sized.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gives away the answer, consistently prompts the student to derive each step, and avoids rewriting or completing the student's work. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student reports being stuck on units in Turn 9, the tutor immediately re-explains the substitution method rather than first asking a diagnostic question to locate the specific confusion. 1.3 is full: the tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., 'share the messy version and we'll find where it goes sideways'). 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the unit-conversion impasse spans Turns 8–10 with the tutor repeating essentially the same approach each time without switching strategy, summarizing, or suggesting moving on. 2.2 and section 3 are full: the tutor stays on-task, maintains supportive tone, and keeps responses concise.
